title: The Deal That Won't Hold
subhead: A US-Iran memorandum of understanding opened the week with markets rallying and ships transiting the Strait of Hormuz — then Iran closed it again on Saturday, Israel kept bombing Lebanon, and the hardest questions were deferred to 60 days of negotiations that began in Switzerland on Sunday. The Fed changed leadership and killed forward guidance. The G7 launched a critical minerals alliance targeting China's dominance. And Anthropic's forced model shutdown made AI sovereignty the week's defining tech story.

sections.heading: The Iran Deal's Four-Day Unraveling
sections.body:
The week began with a memorandum of understanding between the US and Iran — a 14-point framework signed at the G7 in Evian that promised to end more than three months of war, reopen the Strait of Hormuz, and start 60 days of negotiations on nuclear limits, sanctions relief, and Lebanon. Oil prices fell 10%. Ships began transiting the strait. Markets priced in peace.

By Saturday, Iran's joint military command had closed the Strait of Hormuz again — the second closure in a week — citing continued Israeli attacks in Lebanon and what it called US bad faith in implementing the memorandum. The IRGC-linked Tasnim News Agency said the strait should remain shut until all MoU conditions are met, including the release of at least $12 billion in frozen Iranian assets and Israel's withdrawal from Lebanon. CENTCOM countered that 55 merchant ships transited normally on Saturday, moving over 17 million barrels of oil. President Trump posted that there would be 'NO TOLLS' in the strait — except tolls imposed by the United States 'for services rendered.'

In between, the deal came under pressure from every direction. Iran cancelled its delegation's trip to Switzerland for the first round of technical talks on Thursday, then reversed course and sent negotiators on Saturday. Israel launched a new ground offensive in Lebanon on Thursday, then agreed to a ceasefire with Hezbollah on Friday — then resumed strikes on Saturday that killed at least 20 people, according to Lebanon's National News Agency. Neither Israel nor Hezbollah are signatories to the US-Iran deal.

Vice President JD Vance departed for Switzerland on Saturday, joining negotiators Steve Witkoff and Jared Kushner who were already on the ground. Former Pentagon official Mick Mulroy told ABC News that Iran believes it holds the upper hand, with the power to choke off a fifth of global energy supply — a lever it did not exercise before the war.
sections.whyItMatters: The deal's core problem is structural: it trades immediate concessions (Hormuz transit, oil waivers) for deferred negotiations on the hardest issues (nuclear limits, sanctions relief, Lebanon). Iran has already demonstrated it can reverse the opening overnight. Neither of the conflict's other main combatants — Israel and Hezbollah — signed on. The 60-day clock is running, but Iran has already used the strait closure as leverage twice in one week.

sections.heading: Oil's False Bottom and the Physical Market Lag
sections.body:
Oil prices fell roughly 10% across the week on deal headlines, with Brent and WTI settling near pre-war levels. Middle East crude slipped into discounts for the first time since the conflict began. US gas prices dipped below $4 per gallon for the first time since March. Gold dropped nearly 3% for the week.

But the physical market tells a different story. Only 26 vessels transited the Strait of Hormuz on Wednesday, per maritime data firm Windward — far below the 138 daily transits typical before the war. Banks estimate full recovery will take several months. Iran has signaled it may impose 'service fees' on transiting ships after the 60-day toll-free period, permanently raising transit costs. The IEA projects a significant oil supply surplus in 2027 once Hormuz flows normalize — but also confirmed that Russian crude output fell 10% below May targets and roughly 5% year-on-year, the first such decline attributed directly to Ukrainian drone strikes.

The contradiction is built into the deal itself: futures markets are pricing a return to normal that the physical infrastructure cannot yet deliver. Mine clearance, insurance normalization, and Iran's potential toll regime all add friction between the diplomatic headline and the barrel that actually reaches a refinery.
sections.whyItMatters: The gap between financial-market pricing and physical-barrel flows is the deal's most consequential economic feature. Consumers and businesses in oil-importing countries will not see the full benefit of lower futures prices until Hormuz traffic genuinely normalizes — which Iran has now demonstrated it can reverse at will.

sections.heading: The Warsh Fed Kills Forward Guidance
sections.body:
Kevin Warsh's first FOMC meeting as Fed chair kept rates steady but changed almost everything else. The new chair scrapped forward guidance, calling it 'not well suited' to the current moment. He skipped his own dot on the rate-path chart. The policy statement was shorter and deliberately vaguer than under Powell. Five task forces were launched to review the Fed's communications, inflation gauge, and decision-making framework.

Markets repriced aggressively. Two-year Treasury yields surged 13 basis points after the press conference — the largest jump since April 2025. The updated dot plot showed half the committee leaning toward a rate hike by year-end. UBS pushed its expected easing timeline to March 2027, meaning no cuts this year. Money markets now fully price a 25bp hike by October. Former Fed Vice Chair Roger Ferguson told CNBC he still expects a rate hike this year.

The Warsh shift came as global central banks tightened in lockstep despite the Iran deal's oil-price relief. The Bank of Japan raised its benchmark rate to 1% — the highest since 1995 — with further hikes flagged. The ECB hiked and kept a July increase on the table, with ECB Chief Economist Philip Lane warning of a 'mid-sized inflation shock' and Governing Council member Pierre Wunsch saying a July hike remains possible if inflation evidence accumulates. Canada's inflation rose to 3% in May. Indonesia hiked three times in four weeks.

The message across every major central bank: the Iran deal may have eased the energy shock, but the inflation damage is structural, not transient. The Fed's abandonment of forward guidance means markets can no longer discount future easing — a structural shift in how US monetary policy communicates with the world.
sections.whyItMatters: The Fed's communication architecture — built over three decades to reduce market volatility through predictability — has been deliberately dismantled. Combined with a global tightening wave that refuses to wait for the Iran deal to cool inflation, this means the financial impact of the war persists well beyond the ceasefire. The dollar hit a one-year high; gold is testing key support at $4,161.

sections.heading: Israel, Lebanon, and the Deal's Missing Signatories
sections.body:
The US-Iran memorandum folds Lebanon's ceasefire into its framework — but neither Israel nor Hezbollah signed it, and both have repeatedly demonstrated they will operate outside its terms. Israel launched a new ground offensive in Lebanon on Thursday, the same day Iran suspended its Switzerland talks. On Friday, Israel and Hezbollah agreed to a ceasefire. By Saturday, Israeli strikes in southern Lebanon killed at least 20 people, including children, according to Lebanon's National News Agency. Hezbollah said it would abide by the ceasefire but repel Israeli attacks.

Iran used Israel's Lebanon campaign as justification for closing the Strait of Hormuz twice. The deal's 14 points apparently require Israeli de-escalation in Lebanon as an Iranian condition for keeping the strait open — but Washington cannot guarantee Israeli compliance, and Netanyahu's government has shown no willingness to constrain its Lebanon operations to accommodate the US-Iran framework.

The pattern is clear: every Israeli escalation in Lebanon triggers an Iranian response at Hormuz, and every Iranian escalation at Hormuz threatens the broader deal. Lebanon is not a side issue — it is the deal's most vulnerable pressure point.
sections.whyItMatters: A peace deal that depends on the behavior of parties who did not sign it is only as strong as their willingness to cooperate. Israel's military campaign in Lebanon and Iran's Hormuz closures are now directly linked, creating a feedback loop that the 60-day negotiation window may not have time to break.

sections.heading: Ukraine's Drone Campaign Forces Fuel Rationing in Moscow
sections.body:
While the Iran deal dominated headlines, Ukraine's drone war against Russian energy infrastructure produced measurable results. A Ukrainian drone attack on the Gazprom Neft Moscow refinery — potentially the largest drone strike on Moscow in the war — forced Russia to slash fuel quality standards for the domestic market, allowing refineries to produce fuel with lower environmental specifications. President Zelenskiy confirmed Ukrainian drones also struck a refinery in western Siberia's Tyumen region, extending the campaign's reach deep into Russian territory.

The IEA confirmed that Russian crude production fell roughly 10% below May targets, to 8.7 million barrels per day, and approximately 5% year-on-year — the first such decline attributed directly to Ukrainian attacks. This is not a marginal disruption: it represents a measurable contraction of Russia's most important export revenue stream.

Ukraine also asked the US for a license to build Patriot interceptors domestically, a significant escalation in its request for weapons production capability rather than just weapons deliveries. The request comes as the US faces its own interceptor stockpile crisis: the Iran war burned through American air defense munitions, exposing a cost asymmetry that favors cheap drones over expensive interceptors.
sections.whyItMatters: Ukraine is proving that a sustained drone campaign can degrade a major power's energy infrastructure and force domestic rationing. The combination of Ukrainian offensive drone capability and depleted US interceptor stockpiles is reshaping the cost calculus of modern warfare — cheap drones are outproducing expensive missile defenses.

sections.heading: AI Sovereignty: The Anthropic Shutdown and the Scramble for Control
sections.body:
On June 12, the US Commerce Department ordered Anthropic to suspend access to its Fable 5 and Mythos 5 models for all non-US persons, citing national security concerns. Anthropic had roughly 90 minutes to comply. Because it could not technically separate foreign nationals from its global user base in that window, the company shut down both models worldwide — even for its own employees.

Anthropic pushed back publicly, arguing the government's evidence amounted to a narrow, non-universal jailbreak, and warned that applying the same standard across the industry 'would essentially halt all new model deployments for all frontier model providers.' This is the first time the 'deemed export' doctrine — traditionally used for physical technology transfers — has been applied to a commercially deployed AI inference API.

The reaction was swift. French President Emmanuel Macron called the restrictions 'strictly nationalist' and warned that 'nobody will buy US AI if they fear it can be switched off overnight.' European Parliament member Aura Salla said 'Europe cannot keep building its tech stack on access that can be switched off overnight by a foreign government.' At the G7, leaders discussed a 'trusted partners' framework that could give approved countries and companies access to restricted models — but no technical mechanism for discriminated access yet exists.

By week's end, the White House had pivoted from punishing Anthropic to collaborating on AI security standards. A June 2 executive order established a voluntary pre-release review process for frontier models, and POLITICO and Wired reported that the two sides are now working on a framework to evaluate security vulnerabilities. The shift from forced takedown to standards-setting is pragmatic — but it creates an awkward dynamic where the company the government just punished is now helping write the rules.
sections.whyItMatters: The Anthropic order established that a single Commerce Department letter can remove a deployed AI system worldwide in 90 minutes — a new failure mode for every enterprise building on US frontier models. The G7's 'trusted partners' framework is a diplomatic placeholder without a technical implementation, and Macron's pushback signals that allied governments will not passively accept US unilateral control over AI access.

sections.heading: China's Counter-Squeeze: Indium, Stealth, and the AI Price War
sections.body:
Where Washington restricts China's access to advanced chips, Beijing answers with its own leverage. This week, China stepped up customs scrutiny on indium and indium phosphide exports — materials with no ready substitute for the high-speed optical chips that connect GPUs inside AI data centers. InP wafer prices have surged roughly 250%, from about $1,400 to $5,000 per six-inch wafer, since Beijing imposed export controls in early 2025. China controls roughly 70% of global indium production.

Meanwhile, Chinese AI labs are commoditizing model access at a pace that outstrips US export controls. ByteDance launched Seedance 2.0 Mini at $3.40 per million tokens. Xiaomi cut MiMo V2.5 by 99%. Tencent slashed Hy-MT2-Pro by 70%. Alibaba offered 50% off Qwen3.7-Max. Bank of America Securities noted that China's AI landscape has 'limited capability gaps across incumbents,' making price the primary battleground. Hong Kong Science and Technology Park partnered with SenseTime to build a 40,000-petaflop AI data centre by 2030, following a 'train on the mainland, process in Hong Kong, serve the world' model.

Separately, researchers at Foshan University reported a low-cost stealth material made from expanded graphite and titanium dioxide — cheap, readily available materials that could reduce production costs compared to existing coatings reliant on expensive graphene and carbon nanotubes. And US Commerce Secretary Howard Lutnick raised concerns with ASML that an EUV lithography machine may have reached China; ASML firmly denied ever shipping an EUV tool to China.
sections.whyItMatters: China is matching every US chip restriction with a materials countermeasure while simultaneously driving model prices toward zero. The indium squeeze directly targets AI data center build costs. The ASML episode shows Washington is moving from passive export control lists to active verification. And the price war means Chinese models are becoming absurdly cheap precisely when US controls are trying to restrict their reach.

sections.heading: Europe Builds, Starmer Falls, and the G7's Mineral Gambit
sections.body:
At the G7 in Evian, leaders launched the Critical Minerals Resilience and Production Alliance, targeting under 60% dependency on any single non-G7 supplier by 2030, with an ambition of 50%. China currently controls roughly 70% of global refining capacity and 94% of permanent magnet manufacturing. The declaration covers 195 initiatives representing €64 billion in investments, equity stakes, and offtake agreements. Trump's proposed price-floor mechanism for critical minerals — reportedly backed by a Pentagon AI model — faced skepticism from G7 partners and industry. The US also committed $1.2 billion to domestic rare-earth processing through the Pentagon.

Europe's AI infrastructure push moved from pledges to concrete commitments. Mistral AI and Nvidia launched Mistral Compute, a sovereign GPU cloud platform built on 18,000 Grace Blackwell Superchips. Foxconn and Bull announced a partnership to manufacture AI servers in Angers, France, backed by over €120 million in investment. Mistral's 44-megawatt data center in Bruyeres-le-Chatel is deploying its first systems, financed with $830 million in debt. Nvidia CEO Jensen Huang said the company will increase European AI computing capacity tenfold over two years — though Europe currently holds less than 5% of global AI infrastructure capacity in key segments.

In the UK, Prime Minister Keir Starmer is reportedly set to resign on Monday, June 22, after more than 100 ministers called for his resignation and Labour MPs backed Andy Burnham as a replacement. The political crisis comes the same week the UK banned social media for under-16s, became the first country to ban romantic AI chatbots for minors, and seized a Russian shadow fleet tanker in the English Channel — the first military seizure of a Russian vessel by the UK.

The EU finalized its trade deal with the US, with the European Parliament approving it 440-151-50. The deal caps US tariffs on most EU exports at 15%. The US simultaneously delayed blacklisting DeepSeek and over 100 Chinese tech firms ahead of the Trump-Xi meeting — the longest gap in Entity List updates in over a decade.
sections.whyItMatters: The G7 is putting real money — €64 billion — behind supply chain diversification from China, but China has grown its market share in recent years despite Western efforts. The 94% magnet manufacturing share cannot be displaced by policy statements. Europe is finally pouring concrete for sovereign AI infrastructure, but at less than 5% of global capacity, the gap between commitment and capability is enormous. And Starmer's likely resignation adds political instability in a key NATO ally to a week already defined by fragility.

sections.heading: Defense Industry Realignment: FCAS Collapses, Interceptors Run Low
sections.body:
Europe's most ambitious defense cooperation project is over. Germany and France officially pulled the plug on the FCAS sixth-generation fighter program this week, ending years of disagreements over work share, intellectual property, and industrial sovereignty. The collapse leaves both countries without a next-generation fighter plan and raises questions about whether Europe can execute major joint defense programs at all.

Meanwhile, the US faces its own defense readiness crisis. Only 1 in 4 American F-35s is fully mission-capable. The Iran war burned through air defense interceptor munitions, exposing a cost asymmetry that favors cheap drones: the US is spending expensive interceptors to defeat inexpensive threats. Ukraine is now asking the US for a license to build Patriot interceptors domestically — a request that reflects both the urgency of Ukraine's air defense needs and the inadequacy of US production capacity.

South Korea's defense exports are on track to hit $37 billion this year, according to Politico, stepping into the vacuum created by US production shortfalls and European program failures. Poland is steering EU defense loans into its own arms industry. The US Marine Corps is building a permanent war-ready stockpile in southeast Australia — beyond the range of most Chinese missiles — with $30 million allocated for warehouses in Victoria state, expected to reach full capacity by 2028. A similar effort is underway in the Philippines.
sections.whyItMatters: The defense industrial picture is one of fragmentation: Europe can't execute joint programs, the US can't maintain readiness, and South Korea is filling the gap. The interceptor crisis and the FCAS collapse are different symptoms of the same problem — Western defense production has not scaled to match the demands of simultaneous conflicts.

bottomLine: A week that began with a deal and ended with a closure. The US-Iran memorandum of understanding was supposed to stabilize energy markets and open a 60-day negotiation window. By Saturday, Iran had closed the Strait of Hormuz twice, Israel had broken a Lebanon ceasefire it never formally agreed to, and the hardest questions — nuclear limits, sanctions relief, Lebanon — had been deferred to talks that only just started in Switzerland. The deal's architecture trades immediate concessions for future guarantees, and Iran has already demonstrated it can reverse the opening overnight. Meanwhile, the Warsh Fed killed forward guidance and signaled rate hikes are back on the table, central banks worldwide tightened into a slowdown, Ukraine's drones forced fuel rationing in Moscow, and the Anthropic model shutdown made AI access a sovereign issue. The war's consequences have not ended — they have shifted from crisis mode to slow-burn mode, and the institutions designed to manage them are being tested in ways they were not built for.
sources: AP, Reuters, Bloomberg, Wall Street Journal, New York Times, Financial Times, The Economist, TIME, USA Today, CNBC, Seeking Alpha, POLITICO, Wired, SCMP, Nikkei Asia, Straits Times, Business Times Singapore, Arab Times, Kommersant, IEA, OilPrice, FXEmpire, RTE, Business Standard, NDTV Profit, Japan Times, Anthropic, TechTimes, Channel News Asia, Forbes, MSN, AFP, LiveMint, CryptoBriefing, CNN, Politico, Bureau of Investigative Journalism, Reuters Institute, Environmental Science and Ecotechnology, Bank of America Securities, McKinsey, Omdia, Enterprise Singapore
